Hello and welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I sat down for tea with Kevin Sherry, the C.E.O. of Enterprise Ireland, a company that helps Irish businesses start, scale, and grow globally. Sherry and his team are making the rounds along with loads of Irish officials who are in town to tout Irish investment in the U.S.\xe2\x80\x94and who, like the rest of the world, are nervous about how Trump\xe2\x80\x99s tariffs will impact their businesses. It would obviously be better for them if there were fewer barriers to trade; Sherry also noted that supply chains are complicated, and borders matter less and less now, a position that Trump evidently doesn\xe2\x80\x99t agree with.

Mar 12, 2025

Leigh Ann Caldwell

Hello and welcome back to The Best & The Brightest. I\xe2\x80\x99m Leigh Ann Caldwell , back at my computer after Washington\xe2\x80\x99s favorite St. Patrick\xe2\x80\x99s Day tradition: the annual lunch on Capitol Hill where everyone tells stories about how Irish they are. This year, Speaker Mike Johnson hosted President Trump and Irish Taoiseach Miche\xc3\xa1l Martin . Sl\xc3\xa1inte !

Separately, I sat down for tea with Kevin Sherry , the C.E.O. of Enterprise Ireland, a company that helps Irish businesses start, scale, and grow globally. Sherry and his team are making the rounds along with loads of Irish officials who are in town to tout Irish investment in the U.S.\xe2\x80\x94and who, like the rest of the world, are nervous about how Trump\xe2\x80\x99s tariffs will impact their businesses. It would obviously be better for them if there were fewer barriers to trade; Sherry also noted that supply chains are complicated, and borders matter less and less now, a position that Trump evidently doesn\xe2\x80\x99t agree with.

In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s issue, fresh reporting on how angsty Democrats, this time in the Senate, are fretting over their upcoming government funding vote, and what insiders are saying about the woeful state of Democratic leader Chuck Schumer \xe2\x80\x99s\xe2\x80\xa6 leadership . In short, members are confused about how to vote, and craving guidance. So far, they\xe2\x80\x99re not getting it.

But first, speaking of a lack of clarity\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Tariff pandemonium : Trump\xe2\x80\x99s latest round of tariffs against U.S. allies\xe2\x80\x94most recently, a 25 percent duty on steel and aluminum imports that\xe2\x80\x99s prompting retaliatory tariffs from Canada and the E.U.\xe2\x80\x94are making some Republicans nervous. Senator Todd Young , for instance, has asked administration officials for more information about the tariffs, including what other countries can do to avoid them and what the end goal is. \xe2\x80\x9cWe just want clarity on what the future looks like,\xe2\x80\x9d Young told me. But there have been no formal protests from the president\xe2\x80\x99s party\xe2\x80\x94nary even a weakly worded letter to the administration.

    In fact, the House just gave Trump wider latitude to issue tariffs, and made it more difficult for Congress to stop him. Recall that the Constitution says\xe2\x80\x94and at least some Republicans still believe\xe2\x80\x94that imposing tariffs is Congress\xe2\x80\x99s job. (You can read my piece about Rand Paul \xe2\x80\x99s lonely stand on this issue here .) But Trump, and presidents before him, have used national emergency declarations to expand their powers, with sometimes flimsy pretexts. Most recently, Trump has cited border crossings and fentanyl trafficking as a justification for his multifront trade war. In voting to fund the government yesterday, the House changed its rules so that they can\xe2\x80\x99t consider legislation to end those national emergencies for the rest of the year.

    The move was an extreme one, though neither unexpected nor unprecedented. Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 grasp on the House is narrow, and it\xe2\x80\x99s possible that a member of their party ( ahem , Rep. Thomas Massie , perhaps) could join Democrats to try to wrest some control back from the president. G.O.P. members get quite defensive when asked about this latest delegation of their powers, and correctly point out that Democrats passed similar legislation in the past to prevent Republicans from forcing President Joe Biden to end the national Covid emergency. Nevertheless, Trump is managing to get even more power.

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Oscar de la Renta Signature Handbags

Discover More

  • More from Chuck Schumer\xe2\x80\x99s no good, very bad week : It\xe2\x80\x99s not just the funding vote: The leader of the Senate Dems is having a tough time on the political front. Jeanne Shaheen , the moderate, competent New Hampshire senator and former governor who has won four terms as a Democrat in her purplish state, announced today she won\xe2\x80\x99t run again. She\xe2\x80\x99s 78, so this is obviously the correct decision, since too many people age in place in the Senate. But it\xe2\x80\x99s a blow to Schumer, who now will have to spend even more money and time recruiting and electing a Democrat for a seat that\xe2\x80\x99s one of the Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 top pickup opportunities. This after Senator Gary Peters announced his retirement, putting Michigan in play for Republicans, as did Senator Tina Smith in Minnesota, a reliably blue state that Trump has become obsessed with winning.

Now to the main event\xe2\x80\xa6

What the Chuck?

As responsibility for funding the government falls on the Senate, Democrats are growing nervous that Chuck Schumer hasn\xe2\x80\x99t offered them much of a strategy, or a viable off-ramp, to navigate what Republicans are already framing as a possible \xe2\x80\x9cSchumer shutdown.\xe2\x80\x9d

Leigh Ann Caldwell

Chuck Schumer , the typically jovial Senate Democratic leader, has always prided himself on his vintage flip phone and preternatural ability to remember colleagues\xe2\x80\x99 numbers. Lately, however, Schumer hasn\xe2\x80\x99t been rolling calls with the urgency that his caucus has come to expect. Indeed, as Senate Democrats stare down the barrel of their first consequential vote, I\xe2\x80\x99m told Schumer is as torn and demoralized as the rest of his party.

Democrats, of course, have vanishingly little power in Washington these days. One of their fleetingly few points of leverage is the upcoming Senate vote on the Republican bill to fund the government, which passed the House on Tuesday despite near-unified opposition from their party brethren. Its fate now falls to Senate Democrats, at least eight of whom would need to provide the votes to overcome a filibuster. Based on my conversations on the Hill this week, it\xe2\x80\x99s clear that many of them fear being held responsible for a potential government shutdown. At the same time, however, they\xe2\x80\x99re feeling immense pressure from their angry-as-hell constituents to do something about Donald Trump and Elon Musk \xe2\x80\x99s wrecking-ball-style demolition of the federal government, and about looming Republican budget cuts that could impact essential programs like Social Security and Medicaid\xe2\x80\x94Trump\xe2\x80\x99s insistence to the contrary notwithstanding.


In short, it\xe2\x80\x99s the kind of moment that party leaders are made for. Particularly someone like Schumer, who boasts of being in constant contact with his members, weighing their concerns and moods, helping craft strategy\xe2\x80\x94even if members and staff sometimes complain that he\xe2\x80\x99s afraid to play hardball and often defaults to telling members what they want to hear. But these days he doesn\xe2\x80\x99t seem to be telling members much of anything, other than to keep their powder dry and their mouths shut as reporters probe his proclamation that his caucus is unified. (It\xe2\x80\x99s not.)

A MESSAGE FROM OUR SPONSOR

Oscar de la Renta Signature Handbags

Discover More

Ahead of a two-hour, closed-door caucus meeting on Tuesday, members expected to get some answers about how they should vote, and how they should message their votes. Instead, I\xe2\x80\x99m told, they got a grievance-filled brainstorming session in which Schumer did a lot of\xe2\x80\xa6 listening . The meeting ended without a unified message or plan. \xe2\x80\x9cComplete lack of leadership,\xe2\x80\x9d one Democratic aide said. \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s no sense of direction.\xe2\x80\x9d

As early as last week, I began hearing Democrats grumble that Schumer was keeping his cards too close to the vest and offering little insight into his thought process on the funding bill. In hindsight, perhaps, he had no cards to show. Democrats are frustrated that Schumer didn\xe2\x80\x99t ready an aggressive plan to tear apart and criticize the House Republican bill. He didn\xe2\x80\x99t threaten Senate Majority Leader John Thune and his conference with consequences in private, let alone in public.


Instead, Democrats have told me, Schumer\xe2\x80\x99s strategy ahead of the House vote seemed to be to pray that Speaker Mike Johnson couldn\xe2\x80\x99t coalesce his usually divisive Republican conference to pass a government spending bill. When asked what he was telling his members at his weekly news conference on Tuesday\xe2\x80\x94by which point much of the reporting had made clear that the House would pass the bill\xe2\x80\x94Schumer spent barely five seconds saying he\xe2\x80\x99d \xe2\x80\x9cwait and see what the House does,\xe2\x80\x9d before moving on quickly. He apparently didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to believe that the House could fall in line, not because of a Johnson masterstroke, but because of G.O.P. fealty to Trump. Now, funding the government is the Senate\xe2\x80\x99s problem.

To Fund or Not to Fund

In some ways, it seems, Senate Democrats have already lost the messaging war. Republicans have spent days framing a potential shutdown as a \xe2\x80\x9cSchumer shutdown,\xe2\x80\x9d while Schumer has avoided talking about it. Certainly, the issue is hard; the upcoming funding vote could be the most torturous that any Senate Democrat has taken. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re choosing between awful and terrible,\xe2\x80\x9d Senator Raphael Warnock told me, describing the impossible choice between capitulating to Trump and Musk\xe2\x80\x99s cuts, or withholding their votes and likely triggering a government shutdown that would create even more uncertainty for government workers and the country at a time when the White House is not afraid to operate outside the bounds of the law.

The shutdown perils for Dems are substantial, and may ultimately play into Trump and Musk\xe2\x80\x99s hands anyway. Politically, Democrats fear they\xe2\x80\x99ll be blamed, even though Republicans control the government. They\xe2\x80\x99re worried, too, about how they\xe2\x80\x99d get the government back open: Republicans could force them to vote on turning the lights back on one agency at a time\xe2\x80\x94making them decide whether to fund the Federal Aviation Administration, say, without receiving any concessions from Trump. Plus, a shutdown could wreak more havoc on the federal workforce, especially since it would fall to the Office of Management and Budget under Russell Vought \xe2\x80\x94who has long dreamed of eliminating large swaths of the government\xe2\x80\x94to determine which employees are \xe2\x80\x9cessential.\xe2\x80\x9d What if Musk decides to fire all government employees deemed \xe2\x80\x9cnonessential\xe2\x80\x9d and those government workers never get their job back? Wired reported this week that Musk wants a shutdown to more easily fire government employees. Those are the questions that Democrats, and Schumer, are grappling with.

Schumer\xe2\x80\x99s defenders say the leader can\xe2\x80\x99t be heavy-handed, and must consider the demands and concerns of his members as he tries to navigate his caucus through an unprecedented situation with a mercurial president. He\xe2\x80\x99s cautious about careening toward a shutdown when the stakes are so high and the consequences so unpredictable. His members are having to weigh the anger from their animated base against the knowledge that any short-term gain of sticking it to Trump could lead to long-term havoc.

On the other hand, if they vote for Republicans\xe2\x80\x99 funding bill, Democrats are well aware they\xe2\x80\x99ll be basically offering up a \xe2\x80\x9cslush fund\xe2\x80\x9d to Trump with few guardrails on how he can spend the money. They worry that if they don\xe2\x80\x99t take a stand now, then Republicans will continue to roll them at every turn. Senate Democrats are also under pressure from their House counterparts after Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries managed to keep his caucus united in opposition to the bill in the House. \xe2\x80\x9cIf not now, when? If not us, then who?\xe2\x80\x9d California Rep. Scott Peters said in a statement. (Arguably, House Democrats had an easier time holding the line since they could not, by themselves, tank the bill; the dynamics are different in the Senate, where the filibuster prevails.)

Schumer insists his caucus is unified, too, at least on a short-term funding alternative. During another day of closed-door discussions, he told Senate Democrats that the eight Democratic votes necessary to fund the government aren\xe2\x80\x99t there, and he made the same pronouncement on the floor. (Senator Rand Paul has already said he\xe2\x80\x99ll vote against it, which is why Republicans need eight Democrats to advance the bill.) But based on my discussions with Democrats, they aren\xe2\x80\x99t unified on what to do about the vote they actually face. In fact, they\xe2\x80\x99re not unified in their predictions of how many of them will vote for the funding bill\xe2\x80\x94some think there are more than eight \xe2\x80\x9cyea\xe2\x80\x9d votes to fund the government in their midst. Schumer could just be trying to instill fear in Republicans, who are also a bit nervous about how this will all play out, but if there\xe2\x80\x99s a bigger strategy here, it\xe2\x80\x99s a headscratcher.

One Democratic strategist posited to me last week that Senate Democrats would thread the needle, providing the members needed to get past the 60-vote threshold in the procedural votes before voting against actual passage of the bill, which requires only a simple majority\xe2\x80\x94an old-school tactic that lets members claim not to have obstructed process even as they opposed the underlying legislation . But as the public, especially the activated base, understands Senate procedure more than most, it\xe2\x80\x99s a hard needle to thread.

There\xe2\x80\x99s an old adage on Capitol Hill that it\xe2\x80\x99s easier to lead in the minority, when the party out of power has no ability to pass its agenda. But Schumer\xe2\x80\x99s performance on funding is giving Democrats the worst of all governing worlds: They have no power, and, apparently, no leadership either.
